splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous provisions in the Sentencing Guidelines or criminal statutes.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether lenity should trump deference to an agency's interpretation of ambiguous criminal provisions. Several circuits hold lenity prevails, while the Third Circuit (in its panel decision) held that agency deference trumps lenity. The question has significant implications for criminal sentencing uniformity.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity prevails over agency deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, including Sentencing Guidelines, because lenity is a centuries-old canon that prevents agencies from expanding criminal liability without proper lawmaking.

positions.label: Agency deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference (Auer/Kisor deference) to the Sentencing Commission's commentary prevails over the rule of lenity when interpreting ambiguous Sentencing Guidelines provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Sentencing Guidelines Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses (conspiracy, aiding and abetting, attempt) in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference when the guideline text itself does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Sentencing Commission's commentary expanding the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include inchoate offenses deserves controlling deference under Stinson/Kisor. Some circuits defer to the commentary, while others hold it impermissibly expands the guideline text. This affects whether defendants with prior conspiracy convictions qualify as career offenders.

positions.label: Commentary is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of inchoate offenses in 'controlled substance offense' is a permissible interpretation of an ambiguous guideline and is entitled to controlling deference under Stinson and Kisor.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and that the commentary cannot expand the definition beyond the plain text, so Application Note 1 is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to be met before a defendant is disqualified) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: A deep circuit split exists over whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in the safety-valve provision. The Ninth and Eleventh Circuits hold defendants are disqualified only if they meet all three criteria, while the Fifth, Sixth, Seventh, and Eighth Circuits hold that any single criterion disqualifies a defendant. This split causes disparate sentencing outcomes across the country.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required
positions.summary: Holds that 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) is unambiguously conjunctive, meaning defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they satisfy all three criminal-history criteria.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any single criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) is disjunctive, meaning a defendant who meets any one of the three criminal-history criteria is disqualified from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) can be considered as an 'extraordinary and compelling reason' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A), and whether the Sentencing Commission's policy statement (U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6)) permitting such consideration exceeds the Commission's statutory authority.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits (like the Seventh and Eighth) prohibit this, while others allow it. The dispute intensified after the Sentencing Commission amended its policy statement to permit consideration of unusually long sentences resulting from changes in law.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A), and the Sentencing Commission's contrary policy statement exceeds its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as one factor among others when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for compassionate release.

circuits.name: Sixth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Esteras (dissents noting split)

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines (U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b)) when the state's drug schedules cover substances not listed in the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the career-offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally controlled or whether a conviction under any state drug law qualifies. The Eighth Circuit holds there is no federal-law limitation, while other circuits have reached different conclusions. The issue affects whether defendants with state-only drug convictions receive significant sentencing enhancements.

positions.label: No federal-law limitation — state convictions qualify
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline broadly defines 'controlled substance offense' to include offenses under state law, with no textual basis to require the substance to be controlled under federal law.

positions.label: Federal-law limitation applies — substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a controlled substance offense only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal deadline for filing a notice of appeal applies to an order denying a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter (with a 30-day appeal deadline) or a criminal matter (with a 14-day deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies because the motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case. This matters because the shorter criminal deadline could forfeit meritorious appeals.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion arises within the criminal case and is therefore governed by the shorter 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether property appreciation occurring after a Chapter 13 filing but before conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that appreciation inures to the estate, while other circuits have held that debtors retain such appreciation under § 348(f)(1)(A). This split has major practical consequences for debtors whose homes appreciate during bankruptcy.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), coupled with the broad scope of § 541(a), means that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that under the Bankruptcy Code's structure and § 348(f)(1)(A), post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtors who convert in good faith from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release when revoking supervised release, or whether revocation sentences must be based solely on the breach of trust and the need to sanction the violation.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for the underlying conduct that violated supervised release, placing it at what dissenting judges describe as the extreme of a circuit split. Other circuits hold that revocation sentences should focus on the breach of the court's trust, not punishment for the violation conduct itself.

positions.label: Courts may punish for violation conduct
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly impose revocation sentences to punish defendants for the conduct underlying their supervised release violations.

positions.label: Revocation sentences should not punish violation conduct
positions.summary: Holds that the purpose of supervised release revocation is to sanction the breach of the court's trust, not to punish the defendant for the underlying violation conduct.
